

| Create Order service using OutSystems |

[Task 2a Create Order and OrderItem database entities	6](#task-2a-create-order-and-orderitem-database-entities)

[Task 5 – Create API to read a specific order.	30](#task-5-–-create-api-to-read-a-specific-order.)

[Task 6 – Create API to delete a specific order.	36](#task-6-–-create-api-to-delete-a-specific-order.)

[Task 7 – Create API to update a specific order.	42](#task-7-–-create-api-to-update-a-specific-order.)

[Some tips:	52](#some-tips:)

# Learning Objective	

Create an Order microservice using low-code platform OutSystems.

# Prerequisite

Complete “OutSystems Lab Environment Setup Guide”.

# Submission Requirement {#submission-requirement}

Upon successful completion of the lab, fill in and submit the following form:  
[https://smuedu-dev.outsystemsenterprise.com/SMULabUtilities/RegisterStudentProfile](https://smuedu-dev.outsystemsenterprise.com/SMULabUtilities/RegisterStudentProfile)

See [Task 8](#task-8-–-submission-\(penalty-imposed-for-non-submission\)) for submission instructions.

# Activities {#activities}

In this lab you will create an Order service and expose it as a REST API using OutSystems. 

## **Task 1 – Create a new application in OutSystems** {#task-1-–-create-a-new-application-in-outsystems}

1. Log in to your personal environment.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image1]

2. Click on “New Application”.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image2]

3. Click on “From Scratch”.  Then click on “Next”.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image3]

4. Click on “Service”.  Then click on “Next”.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image4]

5. Name the application as “Bookstore Services”.  Then click on “Create App”. 

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image5]

6. Name a new module as **“Order”**, select module type as **“Service”**.  Then click on “Create Module”. 

![][image6]

Note that all the names for the following services, database entities, and attributes are **case-sensitive**.

## **Task 2 – Create database entities and data structures** {#task-2-–-create-database-entities-and-data-structures}

### **Task 2a	Create Order and OrderItem database entities** {#task-2a-create-order-and-orderitem-database-entities}

1. In this task, you will add two database entities manually – Order and OrderItem.  
   1) Select the “Data” tab.   
   2) Right-click on “Database”.    
   3) Click on “Add Entity”  
   4) Rename the new database entity to “Order”.

   

2. Repeat step 1 above to create a new database entity called “OrderItem”

![][image7]

3. Right click on the “Order” entity, and add or edit entity attributes with proper settings.

|   | Rename the primary key (red color) from “Id” to “order\_id”.  |
| :---- | :---- |
|   |   |
|   |   |

4. Add attributes to the Database entity called “OrderItem”.

|  This auto-establishes a foreign-key relationship to the “Order” table. | Rename the primary key (red color) from “Id” to “item\_id”.  ![][image8]  |
| :---- | :---- |
|   |   |

5. Right-click on “Entity Diagrams” and click “Add Entity Diagram”.  
   1) Rename the new Entity Diagram to “Order”.  
   2) Drag both the Database Entities “Order” and “OrderItem” into the canvas.

   

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image9]

**NOTE:** You will see that there is a one-to-many relationship between “Order” and “OrderItem”.  Delete Rule \= Protect also establishes a foreign-key constraint such that no record in the “Order” table can be deleted if there exists a record in the “OrderItem” table having the same value for “order\_id”.

### **Task 2b 	Create input and output data structures** {#task-2b-create-input-and-output-data-structures}

1. Create a Data Structure called *Order\_Item*.  
   1. Right-click the Structures folder and select Add Structure, then name it *Order\_Item.*  (The underscore ensures a different name than OrderItem entity.)  
   2. Expand the Entities folder and locate the *OrderItem* entity.  
   3. Select all the attributes and copy them using the right-click menu or CTRL+C.  
   4. Right-click the *Order\_Item* structure created previously and select *Paste*.

![][image10]

![][image11]

2. Create a Data Structure called *OrderData*.  
   1. Right-click the Structures folder and select Add Structure, then name it *OrderData.*  
   2. Expand the Entities folder and locate the *Order* entity.  
   3. Select all the attributes and copy them using the right-click menu or CTRL+C.  
   4. Right-click the *OrderData* structure created previously and select *Paste*.

![][image12]

![][image13]

5. Right-click the *OrderData* structure and add an attribute called “order\_item” of type Order\_Item List. Select “**List…**”, followed by order\_item.

![][image14]

3. Create a Data Structure called *OrderAPI*.  
   1. Right-click the Structures folder and select Add Structure, then name it *OrderAPI.*  
   2. Right-click the *OrderAPI* structure and add an attribute called “data” of type OrderData. 

![][image15]

**NOTE:** Wrapping the order data inside a json object called “data” like we have done here is required for this lab so that the OutSystems version of the Order service has an identical output format as the Python version of the Order service (to be provided later). If not for this compatibility consideration, the “data” wrapper is not necessary, and OrderData can be used directly as an output of the OutSystems version of the Order microservice.

4. Create a Data Structure called *CartItem*.

![A close up of wordsAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image16]

1. Add the following attributes:

|   |   |
| :---- | :---- |

5. Create a Data Structure called *Data*.

![][image17]

1. Right-click the Structures folder and select Add Structure, then name it *Data.*  
   2. Right-click the *Data* structure and add an attribute called “Orders” of type **OrderData List** (NOT the Order List). To set the data type, select “**List…**”, followed by OrderData.  
        
   3. Put “orders” into the “Name in JSON” field.

![][image18]

6. Create a Data Structure called CreateOrderRequest.

![][image19]

1. Add the following attributes:  
   

|   |   |
| :---- | :---- |

7. Create a Data Structure called UpdateOrderRequest.

![][image20]

1. Add the following attributes:

|   |   |
| :---- | :---- |
|   |  |

8. Create a Data Structure called GetOrdersResponse.

![][image21]

1. Right-click the Structures folder and select Add Structure GetOrdersResponse.  
   2. Right click on GetOrdersResponse and add Structure Attribute, then name it *Data* of type Data.

   Note to put “data” into “Name in JSON” (this is to be compatible with the Python version of the Order microservice later).

![][image22]

9. This step is to add a Server Action called SetStatusCode. This will allow us to set the HTTP return code for our methods.

   1. In the Logic tab, under Server Actions (NOT Service Actions), right-click on System, and select **Manage Dependencies**.

   

![][image23]

2. Under Manage Dependencies, select **HTTPRequestHandler (extension)** on the left navigation window, and then under the right navigation window check the Server Action called **SetStatusCode**, then click Apply. 

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image24]

3. Once complete, you will now see the new server action **SetStatusCode** available for use.

![][image25]

10. Create an Exception called *HandlerErrorException*.  
    1. Under the Logic tab, expand the “Exceptions” folder.  
    2. Under “All Exceptions”, right-click on “User Exception” and click on “Add User Exception”.  
    3. Name the new exception as *HandleErrorException*.

![][image26]

11. Create a general-purpose error handler.  
    1. Under the Logic tab, right-click on “Server Actions” to add a new server action called *HandleError*. Add an Input Parameter called *ErrorDetail*.

|   |   |
| :---- | :---- |

2. Complete the flow logic as follows:

![][image27]

Drag “Raise Exception” to replace the original “End” in the logic flow, Select **HandlerErrorException**, and fill in the Exception Message *ErrorDetails*.

![][image28]

**NOTE:** 

* This is a general-purpose error handler that will be called by all API methods whenever an error occurs.  It is hard coded to return a status code 400\.  If you want more flexibility on the error code, you may choose to add another input parameter to this server action called *StatusCode*.  
* Click the “Publish” button in OutSystems to save your logic flow immediately after resolving any errors as changes are not auto‑saved.

## **Task 3 – Create API to add a new order** {#task-3-–-create-api-to-add-a-new-order}

In this section we will now work on a new API method which will allow us to add a new order including a list of cart items.

1. Right-click on REST and select “Expose REST API” under the Logic tab→Integrations.

Name the REST API as “Order”.

2. Create the *CreateOrder* REST API method to create a new order.

   1) Right-click the *Order* REST API and select **Add REST API Method**  
   2) Set the name of the method to *CreateOrder.*  
   3) Change the **URL Path** to /order/  
   4) Set the HTTP Method to **POST**.

 ![][image29]

5) Right-click the CreateOrder method and select **Add Input Parameter.**

   

   6) Change the name of the input to *Request*, set the Data Type to *CreateOrderRequest* structure, and set “Receive In” to BODY.

 ![][image30]

7) Right-click the CreateOrder method and select **Add Output Parameter**.

   

   8) Change the name of the input to *Response*, set the Data Type to OrderAPI structure.

 ![][image31]

2. Double-click on the CreateOrder method, and complete the logic flow as follows:

![][image32]  
You may find and drag relevant actions from the panel on the left into the logic flow. You may also type partial names to search for an action when a selection dialog box pops up.

|  Click on the \+ button next to Source to expand it and expose the relevant attribute fields. |  Click on the \+ button next to Source to expand it and expose the relevant attribute fields.  |
| :---- | :---- |
|   | ![][image33]  |
| From the *Data* tab, add *Order* database entity to the logic flow.![][image34] To configure Sources and Filters in *GetOrdersByOrderId*, click more options (...) and enter the relevant values. ![][image35] | Add *OrderItem* database entity to the logic flow diagram. Similar to *GetOrdersByOrderId*, click more options (...) to configure Sources and Filters.  ![][image36] Click more options (...) to configure the Filter ![][image37]  |
| ![][image38] |   |

3. Publish the application using 1-Click Publish button and verify it completed successfully in the 1-Click Publish tab.  Ignore the Warning for now.

![][image39]

4. Open Documentation and test the new method using **Postman**.  
     
   1) In the Logic tab, right-click the exposed API named Order, and select **Open Documentation**.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image40]

**NOTE:** When modules containing exposed REST APIs are published, the OutSystems platform server automatically generates the Documentation page. This page contains essential information that can be provided to whoever will integrate with the defined APIs. Although we have not written Descriptions for the API, its methods, and the outputs, defining them will have influence in the generated Documentation. Descriptions, HTTP Methods, URL Paths, and others are used when the Documentation is generated. The generated documentation is available at https://\<yourserver-address\>/\<module-name\>/rest/\<api-name\>/

2) Expand the “POST /order/” method and copy the URL.

![][image41]

3) Open Postman, create a new request, and paste the above URL.

   

   4) Paste the following in the Body:

   

   {

     "customer\_id": "Alice Tan",

     "cart\_item": \[

       {

         "book\_id": 9781449474223,

         "quantity": 1

       },

       {

         "book\_id": 9781449474211,

         "quantity": 2

       }

     \]

   }

![][image42]

5) Add a few more orders, using different values for customer\_id and/or book\_id.

## **Task 4 – Create API to read all orders.** {#task-4-–-create-api-to-read-all-orders.}

Here we will create our next method of the API, named *GetOrders*. This method will allow external systems to retrieve the list of Orders using a REST endpoint and in JSON format.

1. Create the *GetOrders* REST API method to return a list of orders. The output of the

method should contain only a subset of attributes of the Order entity.

1) Right-click the *Order* REST API and select **Add REST API Method**  
2) Set the name of the method to *GetOrders.*  
3) Change the **URL Path** to /order/

![][image43]

2. Define the output of the *GetOrders* API method as a record containing a list of orders.

1) Right-click the *GetOrders* method and select **Add Output Parameter**.  
2) Set the name of the output parameter to *Response* and set its **Data Type** as *GetOrdersResponse*  
   

![][image44]  
 

3. Double-click on the *GetOrders* method, and complete the logic flow as follows:

![][image45]  
NOTE: Publish your logic flow immediately after resolving any errors as changes are not auto‑saved.

|   |  ![][image46]  ![][image47] 	  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  Filters:  IdentifierToLongInteger(OrderItem.order\_id) \= Response.Data.Orders.Current.order\_id  | ![][image48]  |

4. Publish the application using 1-Click Publish button and verify that the publish completed successfully in the 1-Click Publish tab.

![][image49]

5. Open Documentation and test the new method using Postman.

1) In the Logic tab, right-click the exposed API named Order, and select **Open Documentation**.

2) Expand the “GET /order/”  method and copy the URL.

![][image50]

3) Open Postman, create a new request, and paste the above URL.  
   

![][image51]

## **Task 5 – Create API to read a specific order.** {#task-5-–-create-api-to-read-a-specific-order.}

In this section we will now work on a new API method. This new method will allow us to retrieve the information of a single Order based on a given order\_id number. Therefore, we will need to have one input parameter and then based on that search for the order information in the Database and retrieve it.

1. Create the *GetOrder* REST API method to return a specific order.

   1) Right-click the *Order* REST API and select **Add REST API Method**  
   2) Set the name of the method to *GetOrder.*  
   3) Change the **URL Path** to /order/{order\_id}/

 ![][image52]

4) Right-click the GetOrder method and select Add Input Parameter.

   

   5) Change the name of the input to order\_id, set the Data Type to Integer, and set “Receive In” to URL.

   

![][image53]

6) Right-click the GetOrder method and select Add Output Parameter.

   

   7) Set the name of the output parameter to Response and set its Data Type to OrderAPI structure.

 ![][image54]

2. Double-click on the *GetOrder* method, and complete the logic flow as follows:

![][image55]

NOTE: Publish your logic flow immediately after resolving any errors as changes are not auto‑saved.

|  Filters: IdentifierToInteger(Order.order\_id)=order\_id |   |
| :---- | :---- |
|  ![][image56]  |  Filters: IdentifierToLongInteger(OrderItem.order\_id) \= order\_id  |
|   |   |

3. Publish the application using 1-Click Publish button and verify that the publish completed successfully in the 1-Click Publish tab.

![][image57]

4. Open Documentation and test the new method using Postman.

1) In the Logic tab, right-click the exposed API named Order, and select **Open Documentation**.

2) Expand the “GET /order/{order\_id}/” method and copy the URL.

![][image58]

3) Open Postman, create a new request, and paste the above URL. **Replace** {order\_id} with a valid order id (may be like 1, 2, 3, …).  
   

![][image59]

4) Try again with a non-existent order id and see that an error message is returned,

![][image60]

## **Task 6 – Create API to delete a specific order.** {#task-6-–-create-api-to-delete-a-specific-order.}

In this section we will now work on a new API method which will allow us to delete an existing order including all its order items.

1. Create the *DeleteOrder* REST API method to return a specific order.

   1) Right-click the *Order* REST API and select **Add REST API Method**  
   2) Set the name of the method to *DeleteOrder.*  
   3) Change the **URL Path** to /order/{order\_id}/  
   4) Set the HTTP Method to DELETE.

 ![][image61]

5) Right-click the DeleteOrder method and select **Add Input Parameter**.

   

   6) Change the name of the input to *order\_id*, set the Data Type to Long Integer, and set “Receive In” to URL.

   

 ![][image62]

2. Double-click on the *DeleteOrder* method, and complete the logic flow as follows:

![][image63]

NOTE: Publish your logic flow immediately after resolving any errors as changes are not auto‑saved.

|  Filters: IdentifierToInteger(Order.order\_id) \= order\_id  |   |
| :---- | :---- |

| Double-click the *DeleteFromOrderItem* SQL component in the flow logic diagram to get the following screen: Right click “Parameters” to add query parameter *order\_id* Right click “Output Entities / Structures” to add *OrderItem*  DELETE FROM {OrderItem} WHERE {OrderItem}.\[order\_id\] \= @order\_id Type in the SQL statement shown above, and make sure “order\_id” is as shown. ![][image64]       |
| ----- |

| Double-click the *DeleteFromOrder* SQL component in the flow logic diagram to get the following screen: Right click “Parameters” to add query parameter *order\_id* Right click “Output Entities / Structures” to add *Order*                                              DELETE from {Order} WHERE {Order}.\[order\_id\] \= @order\_id  Type in the SQL statement shown above, and make sure “order\_id” is as shown. ![][image65] |
| ----- |

3. Publish the application using 1-Click Publish button and verify that the publish completed successfully in the 1-Click Publish tab.

![][image66]

4. Open Documentation and test the new method using Postman.

1) In the Logic tab, right-click the exposed API named Order, and select Open Documentation.

2) Expand the “DELETE /order/{order\_id}” method and copy the URL.

![][image67]

3) Open Postman, create a new request, and paste the above URL.

![][image68]

4) Click \[Send\] again and see that an error message is returned.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image69]

## **Task 7 – Create API to update a specific order.** {#task-7-–-create-api-to-update-a-specific-order.}

In this section we will now work on a new API method which will allow us to update a specific order including a list of cart items.

1. Create the UpdateOrder REST API method to update a specific order.  
   1) Right-click the Order REST API and select Add REST API Method  
   2) Set the name of the method to UpdateOrder.  
   3) Change the URL Path to /order/  
   4) Set the HTTP Method to PUT.

 ![][image70]

5) Right-click the UpdateOrder method and select Add Input Parameter.  
   6) Change the name of the input to Request, set the Data Type to UpdateOrderRequest structure, and set “Receive In” to BODY.

        

 ![][image71]  
     

7) Right-click the UpdateOrder method and select Add Output Parameter.  
   8) Change the name of the input to Response, set the Data Type to OrderAPI structure.

 ![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image31]

2. Double-click on the UpdateOrder method, and complete the logic flow as follows:

![][image72]

NOTE: 

* Two “End” are allowed, especially when there are different flow paths.  
* Publish your logic flow immediately after resolving any errors as changes are not auto‑saved.

|  Filters: IdentifierToLongInteger(Order.order\_id) \= Request.order\_id      |        ErrorDetail: "Order \["+Request.order\_id+"\] not found"  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  Type in the SQL statement shown above, and make sure “**Request.order\_id**” is as shown. ![][image73]     	 |  |
|       	 	   |  |
|        |        Filters: IdentifierToLongInteger(OrderItem.order\_id) \= Request.order\_id       |
|   | ![][image74] Click on the \+ button next to Source to expand it and expose the relevant attribute fields. |

3. Publish the application using 1-Click Publish button and verify it completed successfully in the 1-Click Publish tab.  Ignore the Warning for now.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image39]

4. Open Documentation and test the new method using Postman.  
          
   1) In the Logic tab, right-click the exposed API named Order, and select Open Documentation.

   2) Expand the “PUT /order/” method and copy the URL.

![][image75]

3) Open Postman, create a new request, and paste the above URL.

   4) Paste the following in the Body:

       

   {

     "order\_id": 2,

     "customer\_id": "Alice Tan",

     "cart\_item": \[

       {

         "book\_id": 9781449474223,

         "quantity": 3

       },

       {

         "book\_id": 9781449474211,

         "quantity": 3

       }

     \]

   }

![][image76]

5) Update a few more orders, using different values.  Add additional cart items and observe that the entire old set is replaced by the entire new set.

## **Task 8 – Submission (Penalty imposed for non-submission)** {#task-8-–-submission-(penalty-imposed-for-non-submission)}

Fill in the Student Profile Registration page in the SMU Lab Utilities upon completion of the lab:  
[https://smuedu-dev.outsystemsenterprise.com/SMULabUtilities/RegisterStudentProfile](https://smuedu-dev.outsystemsenterprise.com/SMULabUtilities/RegisterStudentProfile)

**Penalty will be imposed for non-submission.**

![][image77]

In the form, *Personal Environment Domain Name* refers to your OutSystem development environment URL in the form of [https://xxxxx.outsystemscloud.com](https://xxxxx.outsystemscloud.com). It can be obtained in the OutSystem environment page:

![][image78]

## **Task 9 – Backup / Recover your project \[IMPORTANT for saving your work\]** {#task-9-–-backup-/-recover-your-project-[important-for-saving-your-work]}

If you are implementing the project in your OutSystems personal environment, then you will need to back up your project regularly.

IMPORTANT: OutSystems personal environments are free tier, and OutSystems will cleanup (delete) / recycle personal environments that are dormant after a few weeks. If you are regularly using your personal environment, then your projects will not be deleted. However, just to be safe, it is advised that you backup your projects regularly (perhaps after each coding session).

1. Backup your project.

   1) Click on the far-left tab to go back to your home page.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image79]

2) Or from the home page, click on your project.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image80]

3) Click on the Download button to save the project archive (OAP file) to your laptop.

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image81]

2. In the event that your OutSystems personal environment gets deleted after a few weeks of inactivity, you can import our project archive (OAP) file back into your environment. 

   1) In our home page, click on Environment, then select “Open Files…”.  
   2) Change the file type to OutSystems File.  
   3) Select your OAP file and click Open.  
   4) You will be prompted to install the application and click on “Proceed” to do so.

   

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image82]

# Troubleshooting Guide

## Some tips: {#some-tips:}

* To reduce repetitive work when constructing the logic flows, you may copy the logic flow from one method to another (e.g., copy the flow of GetOrders into GetOrder and then modify as needed.  
* When the Service Studio shows “Error Found”, you may double click on the error message and it will bring you to the location where the configuration may be wrong. Fix errors as soon as they arise, because accumulated errors are harder to troubleshoot.  
* Pay attention to the **case-sensitive** names. A common reason for failed API calls even when the Service Studio does not report errors is mis-typed or missing names.  
* Click the green “Publish” button as frequently as you like to save your changes made in OutSystems as changes are not auto‑saved.  
* Your OutSystems Personal Environment will be paused if it is inactive for a period of time and will be deleted within 15 days unless it is woken up. You may click on the **Wake up my Personal Edition** link in the OutSystems notification email regarding this or browse to [https://www.outsystems.com/Portal/Trial\_Portal](https://www.outsystems.com/Portal/Trial_Portal) and then click on the **Wake up** button to wake it up.  
    
  ![][image83]
